primerTitle: C++ syntax, types & variables — quick recap
primer:
## Program structure
C++ is **statically + strongly typed and compiled** to native machine code: every variable's type is fixed at compile time, conversions are checked, and source is compiled ahead of time (not interpreted). Execution begins at **`main`** — `int main()` or `int main(int argc, char** argv)`; `return 0` signals success, and falling off the end of `main` implicitly returns `0`.

`#include <iostream>` is a **preprocessor** directive that pastes a header's contents in before compilation, exposing `std::cout`/`std::cin`. Angle brackets `<...>` search system paths; quotes `"..."` search your project first. A **namespace** groups names to avoid clashes; the standard library lives in `std` (`std::cout` = `cout` from `std`). `using namespace std;` imports it all (avoid in headers).

## Types & values
**Integers:** `int`, `short`, `long`, `long long` (each with `unsigned`), plus `char` and `bool`. **Floating-point:** `float`, `double`, `long double`. Sizes are implementation-defined (`int` usually 32-bit, `double` 64-bit; `sizeof(char)==1` by definition). `sizeof(T)` is a compile-time constant byte size.

**Initialise with braces** (uniform init): `int x{42};` — braces forbid narrowing (`int x{3.14};` is an error). `int x = 42;` (copy) and `int x(42);` (direct) also work. `int n{};` zero-initialises. Reading an **uninitialised** local is undefined behaviour (not a guaranteed 0).

`const` makes a value read-only after init (run-time-immutable). `constexpr` is stronger: computable at **compile time**, usable where the language demands a constant (array sizes, template args). `auto` deduces the type from the initialiser (`auto i = 0;` is `int`) — still one fixed static type.

A **reference** `int& r = x;` is an alias for an existing object: bound once, never rebound, never null.

`'a'` is a single `char`; `"a"` is a 2-element char array (letter + trailing `'\0'`). Use `std::string` for real text.

## I/O & arithmetic
`std::cout << x;` — `<<` inserts into the output stream; `std::cin >> x;` — `>>` extracts. Calls chain left-to-right. Prefer `'\n'` over `std::endl` (which also **flushes** the buffer — slow in loops). Integer `/` truncates toward zero and `%` gives the remainder; arithmetic stays integer unless an operand is floating-point (use `2.0`).
name: C++ — Syntax, types & variables
description: The starting point of C++: program structure, fundamental types, variables, const, references, type deduction with auto, and basic I/O with streams. Quick recall and concept cards for absolute beginners moving into C++.

cards.term: What runs first in every C++ program?
cards.definition: The `main` function. Execution begins at `int main()` (or `int main(int argc, char** argv)`); returning `0` signals success. Reaching the end of `main` without a `return` implicitly returns `0`.
cards.summary: `int main()` is the entry point; return 0 = success.

cards.definition: It is a preprocessor directive that pastes the contents of the `<iostream>` standard header into your file before compilation, giving you access to `std::cout`, `std::cin` and the stream machinery. Angle brackets search system include paths; quotes (`"..."`) search your project first.
cards.summary: Preprocessor pulls in a header so its declarations are visible.

cards.term: C++ is a statically and strongly typed, compiled language.
cards.summary: True — types are checked at compile time and code is compiled to machine code.
cards.definition: True. Every variable has a type fixed at compile time (static typing), conversions are checked (strong typing), and the source is compiled ahead of time to native machine code rather than interpreted.

cards.term: Name the integer and floating-point fundamental types.
cards.definition: Integers: `int`, `short`, `long`, `long long` (each with an `unsigned` variant), plus `char` and `bool`. Floating-point: `float` (single precision), `double` (double precision) and `long double`. Exact sizes are implementation-defined, but `int` is typically 32-bit and `double` 64-bit.
cards.summary: int/short/long/long long, char, bool; float/double/long double.

cards.term: How do you declare and initialise a variable in modern C++?
cards.definition: Prefer brace (uniform) initialisation: `int x{42};`. You can also write `int x = 42;` or `int x(42);`. Braces additionally forbid narrowing conversions (e.g. `int x{3.14};` is an error), which catches silent data loss.
cards.note: Copy form `int x = 42;` and direct form `int x(42);` also work.

cards.term: The keyword {{const}} makes a variable read-only after initialisation, so the compiler rejects any attempt to modify it.
cards.summary: const = a value that cannot change after it is set.
cards.definition: `const int n = 10;` declares a constant; assigning to `n` later is a compile error. Marking things `const` documents intent and lets the compiler catch accidental mutation.

cards.sub: A) `const` means run-time-immutable; `constexpr` means computable at compile time    B) they are exact synonyms    C) `constexpr` allows modification later, `const` does not    D) `const` only applies to pointers
cards.definition: Correct: A) `const` means run-time-immutable; `constexpr` means computable at compile time. A `const` value just cannot be reassigned, but may be set from a run-time value. A `constexpr` value must be a compile-time constant, so it can be used where the language demands one (array sizes, template arguments).

cards.term: What is a reference in C++?
cards.definition: A reference (`int& r = x;`) is an alias for an existing object — another name for the same storage. It must be initialised when declared, can never be rebound to a different object, and reading or writing through `r` reads or writes `x`. References cannot be null.
cards.summary: An alias for an existing object; bound once, never null.

cards.definition: `auto` asks the compiler to deduce a variable's type from its initialiser: `auto i = 0;` is `int`, `auto d = 3.14;` is `double`. It reduces verbose type names (especially iterators) and prevents accidental type mismatches. The variable still has a single, fixed static type — `auto` is deduction, not dynamic typing.
cards.summary: Compiler deduces the type from the initialiser.

cards.term: Reading a local variable before assigning it a value gives a guaranteed 0.
cards.summary: False — uninitialised locals hold indeterminate values; using them is undefined behaviour.
cards.definition: False. A local variable of fundamental type that is not initialised holds an indeterminate value, and reading it is undefined behaviour. Always initialise — e.g. `int n{};` zero-initialises it.

cards.term: What does `std::cout << x;` do, and what is `<<`?
cards.definition: `std::cout` is the standard output stream; `<<` is the (overloaded) stream-insertion operator that writes `x` to it. Calls chain left to right: `std::cout << "x = " << x << '\n';`. Use `std::cin >> x;` with the extraction operator `>>` to read input.
cards.summary: `<<` inserts into the output stream; `>>` extracts from input.

cards.definition: Both end a line, but `std::endl` also flushes the output buffer every time, which is slow in loops. `'\n'` just writes a newline and lets the stream flush when convenient. Reach for `std::endl` only when you genuinely need an immediate flush.

cards.term: What is the `std::` prefix and what is a namespace?
cards.definition: A namespace groups names to avoid clashes; the standard library lives in `std`. `std::cout` means 'the `cout` from namespace `std`'. `using namespace std;` imports all of it (convenient but pollutes the global scope — avoid in headers).
cards.summary: Namespaces scope names; the standard library lives in `std`.

cards.term: The operator {{sizeof}} yields the size in bytes of a type or object, e.g. `sizeof(double)` is typically 8.
cards.summary: sizeof reports the byte size of a type/object at compile time.
cards.definition: `sizeof(T)` is a compile-time constant giving the number of bytes an object of type `T` occupies. `sizeof(char)` is 1 by definition; other sizes are implementation-defined.

cards.term: What is the difference between `'a'` and `"a"`?
cards.sub: A) `'a'` is a single `char`; `"a"` is a 2-element char array (the letter plus a null terminator)    B) they are identical    C) `'a'` is a string, `"a"` is a char    D) `"a"` is a single char, `'a'` is an int only
cards.definition: Correct: A) `'a'` is a single `char`; `"a"` is a 2-element char array (the letter plus a null terminator). Single quotes make a character literal; double quotes make a C-string literal that includes a trailing `'\0'`. Use `std::string` for real text handling.

cards.term:
Exercise: what does this print?
`int a{5}; int b{2}; std::cout << a / b << ' ' << a % b;`
cards.sub: Work it out from integer-division and modulo rules.
cards.definition: It prints `2 1`. With two `int` operands, `/` is integer division (truncates toward zero), so `5 / 2` is `2`, and `%` is the remainder, so `5 % 2` is `1`. To get `2.5` you would need a floating-point operand, e.g. `a / 2.0`.
cards.summary: `2 1` — integer division truncates; `%` gives the remainder.

cards.term: Exercise: spot the bug. `double avg = (3 + 4) / 2;` — why is `avg` 3, not 3.5?
cards.sub: Think about the types of the operands before the assignment.
cards.definition: `3`, `4` and `2` are all `int`, so `(3 + 4) / 2` is computed entirely in integer arithmetic: `7 / 2` truncates to `3`, and only then is `3` converted to `double`. Force floating-point with `(3 + 4) / 2.0`, which gives `3.5`.
cards.summary: All-int arithmetic truncates before the result is widened; use 2.0.
